core_takeaway: Agent_1 looked strongly advantaged by moving first: with identical preferences across agents, it named a baseline that gave itself Jewel plus both filler items, and the later speakers accepted Pencil and Apple while deferring any fight over Jewel to hypothetical later rounds that never occurred.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. The final rank order mirrors the speaking order: Agent_1 got Jewel, Stone, and Quill for 48; Agent_2 got Pencil for 29; and Agent_3 got Apple for 23. The counterpoint is that Agent_3 did try to preserve future leverage in voting, but it accepted one identical Round 1 proposal, so that leverage did not change the outcome.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by assigning Jewel and the filler items to itself. Agent_2 and Agent_3 both wanted Jewel too, but publicly accepted the baseline and shifted conflict into future renegotiation language.
phase_notes.private_thinking: The private-thinking entries reinforced rather than challenged the opening baseline. All agents framed Round 1 as maintaining Jewel with Agent_1, Pencil with Agent_2, and Apple with Agent_3, with later changes constrained by no-worse-off guardrails.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All three formal proposals were identical and used Agent_1's opening split. This eliminated any live alternative before voting and made the first anchor the only concrete allocation on the table.
phase_notes.private_voting: Raw votes show Agent_1 accepted because the split gave it 48 utility, Agent_2 accepted because it locked Pencil, and Agent_3 accepted Proposal #1 because it gave Apple. Agent_3 rejected duplicate proposals for flexibility, but the accepted identical proposal already locked the outcome.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in the transcript.

mechanisms.theme: First anchor claimed the top item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to claim Jewel, the universally top-valued item, and added Stone and Quill as fillers. That made the best item part of the starting baseline rather than an open contest.

evidence.quote: Stone and Quill go to me
Jewel is my clear highest target.

mechanisms.explanation: All three agents had the same item values, but Agent_2 treated Pencil as acceptable and Agent_3 treated Apple as reasonable once Agent_1's Jewel claim was on the table.

evidence.quote: I can accept your proposed base split
That baseline gives me Apple now

mechanisms.explanation: The discussion repeatedly postponed fights over Jewel and Pencil to later rounds. Because consensus was reached in Round 1, those promised later opportunities never constrained Agent_1's immediate gain.

evidence.quote: I will not move Jewel in Round 1

mechanisms.theme: Private plans preserved the opening baseline
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private-thinking sections did not reveal hidden plans to overturn Agent_1's claim. They converged on maintaining the same Round 1 allocation and treating Jewel movement as unlikely under guardrails.

evidence.quote: The baseline assigns Jewel to Agent_1
the common ground that minimizes immediate conflict

mechanisms.theme: Identical proposals and votes locked the first split
mechanisms.explanation: Every formal proposal copied the same allocation. Voting then rewarded each agent's assigned lower-ranked item, with Agent_1 explicitly recognizing the allocation as its best immediate utility.

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker had procedural leverage but not allocation leverage
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 did not blindly accept every duplicate proposal; it rejected two identical copies to preserve flexibility. But because it accepted Proposal #1 and all proposals were the same, that procedural move did not alter the first-speaker allocation.
